title: Reboot fails when machine is locked (error 1271 - missing /f flag)
description:
## Bug

When the machine is locked at the time the script attempts to reboot, `shutdown.exe` fails with error 1271:

```
The machine is locked and cannot be shut down without the force option.(1271)
```

This leaves the cycle stuck — updates completed, scheduled task registered, but no reboot happens.

## Root cause

`Invoke-BootUpdateCycle.ps1` line 1017 calls:

```powershell
& shutdown.exe /r /t $script:RebootDelaySec /c $shutdownComment /d p:2:17
```

The `/f` (force) flag is missing. Windows requires `/f` to restart a locked machine.

## Expected behavior

The script should force the restart since it's an automated update cycle. The user already had the opportunity to cancel via the reboot warning / `shutdown /a`.

## Suggested fix

Add `/f` to the shutdown command:

```powershell
& shutdown.exe /r /f /t $script:RebootDelaySec /c $shutdownComment /d p:2:17
```

## Log excerpt

```
[2026-04-25 15:30:37] [Info] Initiating shutdown /r /t 0 | Cancel: shutdown /a
The machine is locked and cannot be shut down without the force option.(1271)
[2026-04-25 15:30:37] [Info] Shutdown scheduled. Exiting; will resume after reboot.
```

Note: the script logs "Shutdown scheduled" even though shutdown.exe failed — exit code is not checked.

## Secondary issue

The script does not check the exit code of `shutdown.exe`, so it reports success and exits even when the reboot was never initiated.
close_reason: Closed on GitHub (gh-7) — backfilled into beads

title: Enhancement: Parallelize PowerShell module updates
description:
## Goal

PowerShell module update phase processes modules sequentially inside a single child job — slow on machines with many installed modules.

## Approach

Use ``ForEach-Object -Parallel`` (PS 7.0+ built-in) inside the existing ``Start-Job`` child process to run N module updates concurrently.

- Throttle: ``[Math]::Min(8, [Math]::Max(2, [Environment]::ProcessorCount))``
- Apply to both the PSResourceGet primary path and the legacy ``Update-Module`` fallback
- Preserve existing structured output format (``UPDATED|name|old|new``, ``ERROR|name|msg``)
- Overall timeout via ``PackageTimeoutMinutes`` instead of per-module timeout
close_reason: Closed on GitHub (gh-38) — backfilled into beads

title: Bug: BBS splash renders blank on Server 2016 / older conhost (encoding)
description:
## Symptom

When launched via ``upd.cmd`` on Windows Server 2016, the ``Show-StartupArt`` BBS splash renders as empty lines. The lean cyan ``Show-CycleBanner`` (using ASCII ``=``/``-``) renders fine; only the Unicode-heavy splash is affected.

## Root Cause

cmd.exe defaults to OEM/ANSI code page (CP437 or CP1252). When ``pwsh`` is launched as a child, ``[Console]::OutputEncoding`` inherits that code page (verified: ``ibm437``). Block-drawing chars (``█`` U+2588, ``╗`` U+2557, ``═`` U+2550, ``░▒▓`` U+2591-2593) get stripped on output.

## Fix

Force UTF-8 console I/O at the top of ``Invoke-BootUpdateCycle.ps1``:

- ``[Console]::OutputEncoding = UTF8``
- ``[Console]::InputEncoding = UTF8``
- ``chcp 65001``

Wrapped in ``try/catch`` so SYSTEM scheduled-task runs (no console attached) don't error.

Tested on Windows Server 2016.
close_reason: Closed on GitHub (gh-37) — backfilled into beads

title: Enhancement: Optional system restore point on first iteration
description:
## Feature

Opt-in Checkpoint-Computer snapshot before first cycle iteration. Tolerates SYSTEM context and Server SKUs (System Restore disabled — logs warning, continues).

Off by default (-SkipRestorePoint:$false to enable).

Shipped in v2.1.
close_reason: Closed on GitHub (gh-36) — backfilled into beads

title: Enhancement: WhatIf / dry-run mode
description:
## Feature

-WhatIf parameter ([CmdletBinding(SupportsShouldProcess)]) on all external mutating calls — package installs, reboots, scheduled task changes, restore points. Zero side effects when set; banner displays [WHATIF - NO CHANGES] tag.

Shipped in v2.1.
close_reason: Closed on GitHub (gh-35) — backfilled into beads

title: Enhancement: Staged rollout (one package manager per boot)
description:
## Feature

-StagedRollout runs only ONE package manager per iteration, then reboots. State persists which manager ran last; next boot picks up next manager. Isolates failure domains for problem-prone environments.

Shipped in v2.1.
close_reason: Closed on GitHub (gh-34) — backfilled into beads

title: Enhancement: Hour-based maintenance window with midnight crossing
description:
## Feature

-MaintenanceWindowStart / -MaintenanceWindowEnd (hours 0-23, -1 = no restriction). Defers (does NOT fail) when outside window. Supports midnight-crossing (e.g., Start=22, End=2 = 10 PM to 2 AM).

Shipped in v2.1.
close_reason: Closed on GitHub (gh-33) — backfilled into beads

title: Enhancement: Exclude packages by substring pattern
description:
## Feature

-ExcludePatterns @('SQL', 'Citrix') skips matching packages across Winget, Chocolatey, and Windows Update phases. Substring match (case-insensitive).

Shipped in v2.1.
close_reason: Closed on GitHub (gh-32) — backfilled into beads

title: Enhancement: Post-update health check with service recovery
description:
## Feature

After all package phases, validate critical services (W32Time, WinDefend, Dnscache, Spooler, EventLog). Auto-restart if stopped, log results to state file, surface in completion notification.

Default: enabled (-SkipHealthCheck to disable).

Shipped in v2.1.
close_reason: Closed on GitHub (gh-31) — backfilled into beads

title: Enhancement: Trend visualization (Show-BootUpdateHistory.ps1)
description:
## Feature

Standalone read-only viewer for cycle history with multiple output formats.

- -Format Table — tabular cycle summaries
- -Format Graph — ASCII bar charts with ANSI colors
- -Format JSON — raw structured output

Reads BootUpdateCycle.history.json; no elevation required.

Shipped in v2.1.
close_reason: Closed on GitHub (gh-30) — backfilled into beads

title: Enhancement: Webhook and email notifications
description:
## Feature

Add post-cycle and reboot-warning notifications via webhook (Teams/Slack/Discord with auto-detection) and email (SMTP, optional auth, async send, proxy support).

## Parameters

- -WebhookUrl — auto-detects Teams/Slack/Discord by URL pattern, formats payload accordingly
- -NotifyEmail / -SmtpServer / -SmtpCredential — SMTP delivery

Shipped in v2.1.
close_reason: Closed on GitHub (gh-29) — backfilled into beads

title: Enhancement: Show BBS startup splash on every iteration
description:
## Problem

`Show-StartupArt` (the BBS-style ASCII splash with the BOOT block letters) only renders on the first iteration of a cycle, gated by ``if (\$isFirstIteration)`` in ``Invoke-BootUpdateCycle``. After a reboot — or on any re-run where ``\$state.StartTime`` is already populated — the user only sees the lean ``Show-CycleBanner`` cyan bar, no splash.

## Desired Behavior

Splash should render on every iteration: every ``upd.cmd`` invocation, every post-reboot SYSTEM run.

## Fix

Drop the ``\$isFirstIteration`` gate; always call ``Show-StartupArt`` before ``Show-CycleBanner``.
close_reason: Closed on GitHub (gh-28) — backfilled into beads

title: Bug: Banner version hardcoded, shows v2.1 instead of current release
description:
## Description

Banner version strings were hardcoded as 'v2.1' in three places. Not updated when v2.2.0+ shipped.

## Fix

Extracted to \`\$script:BootUpdateCycleVersion\` variable (single source of truth). All three banner strings now reference it.

**Fixed in:** v2.3.2
close_reason: Closed on GitHub (gh-27) — backfilled into beads

title: Enhancement: Comprehensive pending reboot detection (Boxstarter-style)
description:
## Description

Enhanced `Test-PendingReboot` with all checks from Brian Wilhite's `Get-PendingReboot` (used by Boxstarter):

| Check | Before | After |
|-------|--------|-------|
| CBS RebootPending | Property lookup | Subkey existence (Test-Path) |
| WU RebootRequired | Property lookup | Subkey existence (Test-Path) |
| PendingFileRenameOperations | Property exists | Verify entries count > 0 |
| Netlogon JoinDomain | Property only | JoinDomain + AvoidSpnSet |
| ComputerRename | Present | Unchanged |
| SCCM CCM_ClientUtilities | Missing | Added (WMI, graceful skip) |

SCCM check silently returns false when the CCM client isn't installed.

**Fixed in:** v2.3.1 (2d0185a)
close_reason: Closed on GitHub (gh-26) — backfilled into beads

title: Bug: PackageManagement 'currently in use' warning clutters logs
description:
## Description

During module updates, the log fills with:

```
WARNING: The version '1.4.8.1' of module 'PackageManagement' is currently in use. Retry the operation after closing the applications.
```

This is a known, benign warning when `PackageManagement` is loaded in the parent PowerShell session. It does not affect update functionality.

## Fix

Added log filter to suppress this warning pattern.

**Fixed in:** v2.2.1 (6c51d65)
close_reason: Closed on GitHub (gh-25) — backfilled into beads

title: Bug: Az meta-module exceeds 5-minute per-module timeout
description:
## Description

`Az` is a wrapper meta-module containing 80+ sub-modules. `Update-Module -Name Az` attempts to resolve and update all sub-modules recursively, exceeding the 5-minute per-module timeout:

```
TIMEOUT: Az exceeded 5m
```

The sub-modules (`Az.Accounts`, `Az.Advisor`, etc.) are already individually enumerated by `Get-InstalledModule` and update fine on their own.

## Fix

Exclude the `Az` meta-module from the generic update loop. Sub-modules update individually within their own timeout windows.

**Fixed in:** v2.2.1 (6c51d65)
close_reason: Closed on GitHub (gh-24) — backfilled into beads

title: Bug: AWS.Tools.* modules fail with stale temp repository error
description:
## Description

All `AWS.Tools.*` modules fail during the PowerShell module update phase with:

```
Unable to find repository 'C:\Users\fflintstone\AppData\Local\Temp\hi02t1b2.1r0'
```

`AWS.Tools.Installer` registers a temp NuGet repository during its operations. When `Update-Module` later tries to update `AWS.Tools.*` modules, it references this stale temp path that no longer exists.

## Fix

Skip `AWS.Tools.*` from the generic `Update-Module` loop. Use `Update-AWSToolsModule -CleanUp` from `AWS.Tools.Installer` instead — it handles dependency ordering, updates, and old version cleanup correctly.

**Fixed in:** v2.2.1 (6c51d65)
close_reason: Closed on GitHub (gh-23) — backfilled into beads

title: Enhancement: Migrate module updates to PSResourceGet with bulk Update-PSResource
description:
## Description

Replace the sequential `Start-Job { Update-Module }` loop (139 modules × 5min timeout each) with `Update-PSResource -Name *` for a single bulk update call. PSResourceGet is C#-native, ~2x faster, and eliminates PackageManagement dependency conflicts.

## Current Implementation

```powershell
foreach ($mod in $modules) {
    $job = Start-Job { Update-Module -Name $modName -Force }
    Wait-Job -Timeout ($perModTimeout * 60)
    # ... per-module error handling
}
```

**Problems:** 139 sequential gallery round-trips, Start-Job spawns full pwsh.exe per module (~200-500ms overhead each), PackageManagement conflicts, orphan processes on timeout.

## Proposed Implementation

```powershell
# Prefer PSResourceGet (bundled with PS 7.4+)
if (Get-Command Update-PSResource -EA SilentlyContinue) {
    $job = Start-Job {
        Update-PSResource -Name * -Scope AllUsers -Force -AcceptLicense `
            -TrustRepository -Quiet -PassThru 2>&1
    }
    $done = $job | Wait-Job -Timeout (30 * 60)  # Single 30min timeout for all
    # ... handle results
} else {
    # Fallback: existing per-module Update-Module logic
}
```

## Key Benefits

- **Single call** replaces 139 sequential round-trips
- **Child process** avoids module-in-use file locks
- **Dependency-aware** — handles AWS.Tools.Common → AWS.Tools.* ordering automatically
- **No PackageManagement** — PSResourceGet bypasses it entirely
- **2x faster** in benchmarks

## Research Sources

- PSResourceGet GA since Oct 2023, current stable **1.1.1** (March 2025)
- Friedrich Weinmann PSConfEU 2025: "Package Management: The Way We Always Wanted It To Be"
- Jeff Hicks recommends migration in "PowerShell Refresh" blog post
- JustinGrote's optimized version-check gist for pre-filtering
- PSGallery rate limits at ~10-20 concurrent API calls; `Update-PSResource -Name *` serializes internally

## Considerations

- Requires `Microsoft.PowerShell.PSResourceGet` 1.1.1+ (bundled with PS 7.4+)
- Old module versions are installed side-by-side (not removed) — may want cleanup phase
- `AWS.Tools.*` should use `Update-AWSToolsModule -CleanUp` from `AWS.Tools.Installer`
- Keep fallback to `Update-Module` for PS 7.0-7.3 compatibility
close_reason: Closed on GitHub (gh-22) — backfilled into beads

title: Enhancement: Randomize ASCII art splash screen colors
description:
## Description

Add subtle color randomness to the BOOT ASCII art splash screen displayed on first iteration. Currently uses fixed cyan/white/dark-gray scheme. Each run should feel slightly different while maintaining readability.

## Scope

`Show-StartupArt` function in `Invoke-BootUpdateCycle.ps1`.

## Constraints

- Keep text legible (avoid dark-on-dark)
- Subtle variation, not carnival
- ANSI colors only (PS7+ compatible)
- Deterministic fallback if host doesn't support colors
close_reason: Closed on GitHub (gh-21) — backfilled into beads

title: Enhancement: Webhook notification retry on transient failure
description:
## Description

Webhook notifications (`Invoke-RestMethod`) have no retry logic. If the POST fails due to a transient network error (timeout, 503, DNS hiccup), the notification is silently lost.

```powershell
$response = Invoke-RestMethod -Uri $url -Method Post -Body $jsonBody `
    -ContentType 'application/json' -TimeoutSec 10 -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue
```

## Suggested Fix

Add simple retry with backoff (e.g., 3 attempts with 2/4/8 second delays). Keep fail-forward behavior — exhausted retries should log a warning but never abort the cycle.

## Severity

LOW — notifications are informational. The cycle completes regardless. History and event log provide alternative audit trails.
close_reason: Closed on GitHub (gh-20) — backfilled into beads

title: Bug: Stop-Job doesn't kill child pwsh process for module updates
description:
## Description

PowerShell module updates use `Start-Job` with `Wait-Job -Timeout`. When a module update times out, `Stop-Job` is called, but this only stops the PowerShell job — the underlying child `pwsh.exe` process may continue running as an orphan.

```powershell
$job = Start-Job -ScriptBlock { param($N) Update-Module -Name $N -Force -EA Stop 2>&1 } -ArgumentList $modName
$done = $job | Wait-Job -Timeout ($perModTimeout * 60)
if (-not $done) {
    $job | Stop-Job -PassThru | Remove-Job -Force  # Job stops, but child process may survive
}
```

## Suggested Fix

After `Stop-Job`, enumerate and kill child processes of the job's process:
```powershell
if (-not $done) {
    $childPid = $job.ChildJobs[0].Information[0]  # or track via ProcessId
    $job | Stop-Job -PassThru | Remove-Job -Force
    Get-Process -Id $childPid -EA SilentlyContinue | Stop-Process -Force
}
```

Or use the existing `Invoke-PackageManagerWithTimeout` function which has robust process tree management.

## Severity

MEDIUM — orphan processes consume resources but are cleaned up on next reboot. Only triggers when a module update hangs past the 5-minute per-module timeout.
close_reason: Closed on GitHub (gh-19) — backfilled into beads

title: Enhancement: SMTP authentication support for email notifications
description:
## Description

Email notifications via `Send-MailMessage` currently have no SMTP authentication support. If the SMTP server requires credentials, notifications silently fail.

The code contains a comment acknowledging this:
```powershell
<# WARNING: This sends credentials in clear text if SmtpServer requires auth.
   Defer auth support to future iteration (SecretManagement/PSCredential). #>
```

## Suggested Approach

- Support `PSCredential` parameter for SMTP auth
- Optionally integrate with `SecretManagement` module for credential retrieval
- Consider deprecating `Send-MailMessage` in favor of direct SMTP client for modern auth (OAuth2)

## Severity

LOW — documented limitation. Most deployment scenarios use internal SMTP relays that don't require auth.
close_reason: Closed on GitHub (gh-18) — backfilled into beads

title: Enhancement: Validate WebhookUrl parameter format
description:
## Description

The `WebhookUrl` parameter accepts any string with no validation. Invalid URLs are silently accepted and fail at runtime when `Invoke-RestMethod` is called.

Additionally, if the URL contains PowerShell special characters, it could be misinterpreted when passed through scheduled task argument strings.

## Suggested Fix

Add parameter validation:
```powershell
[ValidateScript({ $_ -eq '' -or $_ -match '^https?://' })]
[string]$WebhookUrl = ''
```

## Severity

LOW — invalid URLs fail gracefully (caught by try/catch, logged as warning, never aborts cycle).
close_reason: Closed on GitHub (gh-17) — backfilled into beads

title: Bug: ExcludePatterns with special chars break scheduled task argument parsing
description:
## Description

Both `Deploy-BootUpdateCycle.ps1` (line 415) and `Invoke-BootUpdateCycle.ps1` (line 1179) serialize ExcludePatterns for scheduled task arguments using simple single-quote wrapping:

```powershell
$patternStr = ($Config.ExcludePatterns | ForEach-Object { "'$_'" }) -join ','
$taskArgs += "-ExcludePatterns @($patternStr)"
```

If a pattern contains single quotes, commas, parentheses, dollar signs, or backticks, the command-line argument will be misinterpreted or fail to parse when the scheduled task invokes PowerShell.

**Examples that break:**
- `O'Reilly` → `-ExcludePatterns @('O'Reilly')` — unbalanced quotes
- `$(calc)` → potential command substitution
- `Test,Value` → PowerShell interprets as two array elements

## Suggested Fix

Use proper escaping (double single-quotes inside single-quoted strings) or serialize via JSON:
```powershell
$escapedPatterns = $Config.ExcludePatterns | ForEach-Object { $_ -replace "'", "''" }
```

## Severity

MEDIUM — only triggers with unusual characters in package name patterns. Common patterns like `Teams`, `OneDrive`, `SQL` are unaffected.
close_reason: Closed on GitHub (gh-16) — backfilled into beads

title: Bug: MaintenanceWindow parameters accept invalid hour values
description:
## Description

`MaintenanceWindowStart` and `MaintenanceWindowEnd` accepted any integer with no validation. Values like `-999` or `25` were silently accepted, producing unpredictable maintenance window behavior.

## Fix

Added `[ValidateRange(-1, 23)]` to both parameters:
```powershell
[ValidateRange(-1, 23)]
[int]$MaintenanceWindowStart = -1,
[ValidateRange(-1, 23)]
[int]$MaintenanceWindowEnd   = -1
```

**Fixed in:** v2.1.4 (6fb1f95)
close_reason: Closed on GitHub (gh-15) — backfilled into beads

title: Bug: Show-BootUpdateHistory crashes on empty JSON array
description:
## Description

If `BootUpdateCycle.history.json` contains `[]`, `ConvertFrom-Json` returns `$null`. Wrapping in `@($null)` produces an array with `.Count = 1`, bypassing the empty-check on line 93 and crashing downstream code.

## Fix

Filter nulls and add defensive empty check:
```powershell
$allEntries = @($raw | ConvertFrom-Json) | Where-Object { $null -ne $_ }
...
if (-not $allEntries -or $allEntries.Count -eq 0) {
```

**Fixed in:** v2.1.4 (6fb1f95)
close_reason: Closed on GitHub (gh-14) — backfilled into beads

title: Bug: ExcludePatterns uses -like wildcard matching instead of substring
description:
## Description

`Invoke-BootUpdateCycle.ps1` lines 705 and 780 use `-like "*$pattern*"` for ExcludePatterns matching. The `-like` operator treats `[]`, `?`, and `*` as wildcards.

**Example:** Pattern `Office[365]` matches `Office3`, `Office6`, `Office5` instead of the literal substring `Office[365]`.

## Root Cause

```powershell
if ($pkgId -like "*$pattern*") { ... }    # Winget (line 705)
if ($pkgName -like "*$pattern*") { ... }  # Chocolatey (line 780)
```

## Fix

Switched to `.IndexOf()` with `OrdinalIgnoreCase` for true substring matching:
```powershell
if ($pkgId.IndexOf($pattern, [System.StringComparison]::OrdinalIgnoreCase) -ge 0) { ... }
```

**Fixed in:** v2.1.4 (6fb1f95)
close_reason: Closed on GitHub (gh-13) — backfilled into beads

title: Bug: Deploy drops MaintenanceWindow params from scheduled task
description:
## Description

`Deploy-BootUpdateCycle.ps1` `Register-ScheduledTaskNow` never adds `MaintenanceWindowStart` or `MaintenanceWindowEnd` to `$taskArgs`. These parameters are in `$invokeArgs` (lines 232-233) and work for the direct first run, but are silently dropped after reboot.

**Impact:** Maintenance window is completely ignored when running via scheduled task. Updates could run at any time, defeating the purpose of the feature.

## Fix

Added conditional forwarding:
```powershell
if ($Config.MaintenanceWindowStart -ge 0) { $taskArgs += "-MaintenanceWindowStart $($Config.MaintenanceWindowStart)" }
if ($Config.MaintenanceWindowEnd   -ge 0) { $taskArgs += "-MaintenanceWindowEnd $($Config.MaintenanceWindowEnd)" }
```

**Fixed in:** v2.1.4 (6fb1f95)
close_reason: Closed on GitHub (gh-12) — backfilled into beads

title: Bug: Deploy drops SkipHealthCheck when registering scheduled task
description:
## Description

`Deploy-BootUpdateCycle.ps1` `Register-ScheduledTaskNow` builds `$taskArgs` but omits `SkipHealthCheck`. The parameter is correctly forwarded via `$invokeArgs` splatting for the direct first run, but silently dropped when the scheduled task runs after reboot.

**Impact:** Scheduled task always runs health checks even if `$Config.SkipHealthCheck = $true`.

## Fix

Added to `$taskArgs` after `SkipRestorePoint`:
```powershell
if ($Config.SkipHealthCheck) { $taskArgs += '-SkipHealthCheck' }
```

**Fixed in:** v2.1.4 (6fb1f95)
close_reason: Closed on GitHub (gh-11) — backfilled into beads

title: Bug: System.__ComObject noise in Windows Update logs
description:
## Description

PSWindowsUpdate emits COM objects during download/install phases. Their `.ToString()` renders as `System.__ComObject` instead of meaningful text, cluttering the log:

```
[Info] System.__ComObject
[Info] Accepted [1] Updates ready to Download
[Info] System.__ComObject
[Info] Downloaded [1] Updates ready to Install
[Info] System.__ComObject
```

## Root Cause

`Get-WindowsUpdate` pipeline output includes raw COM objects. The `Write-Log $_.ToString()` call logs the type name.

## Fix

Filter out lines where `.ToString()` equals `System.__ComObject`:
```powershell
$line = $_.ToString()
if ($line -eq 'System.__ComObject') { return }
```

**Fixed in:** v2.1.3 (66c66dd)
close_reason: Closed on GitHub (gh-10) — backfilled into beads

title: Bug: Banner version strings still showing v2.0
description:
## Description

All three banner version strings in `Invoke-BootUpdateCycle.ps1` displayed `v2.0` after the v2.1 release:

1. ASCII splash art (first boot) — line 1418
2. Text banner (normal mode) — line 1488
3. Text banner (WhatIf mode) — line 1486

## Fix

Updated all three occurrences from `v2.0` to `v2.1`.

**Fixed in:** v2.1.2 (8a02f42)
close_reason: Closed on GitHub (gh-9) — backfilled into beads

title: Bug: Windows Update NotTitle parameter type error (array vs string)
description:
## Description

`Get-WindowsUpdate -NotTitle` is typed `[String]`, not `[String[]]`. The v2.1 ExcludePatterns integration passed an array (`@('SQL') + $script:ExcludePatterns`), causing:

```
Cannot convert 'System.Object[]' to the type 'System.String' required by parameter 'NotTitle'. Specified method is not supported.
```

Windows Update phase silently failed with 0 packages on every run.

## Root Cause

`Invoke-BootUpdateCycle.ps1` line 819:
```powershell
NotTitle = (@('SQL') + $script:ExcludePatterns)  # Array, but -NotTitle expects String
```

## Fix

Join with `|` to produce a regex alternation string:
```powershell
NotTitle = ((@('SQL') + $script:ExcludePatterns) -join '|')
```

**Fixed in:** v2.1.1 (ce455d7)
close_reason: Closed on GitHub (gh-8) — backfilled into beads

title: Bug: Repair-AwsTooling passes unsanitized UninstallString to cmd.exe
description:
## Description

`Repair-AwsTooling.ps1` reads the AWS CLI `UninstallString` from the registry and passes it directly to `cmd.exe /c` via string interpolation. A crafted registry entry containing shell metacharacters (`&`, `|`, etc.) would execute arbitrary commands.

## Impact

**Low in practice** — requires prior registry compromise, and the script already runs elevated. Flagging for defense-in-depth.

## Location

`Repair-AwsTooling.ps1` line 187:

```powershell
Start-Process cmd.exe -Wait -ArgumentList "/c $cmd"
```

## Suggested Fix

Use the MSI uninstall path directly rather than shelling out through `cmd.exe`, or validate the string matches expected MSI patterns before execution.
close_reason: Closed on GitHub (gh-6) — backfilled into beads

title: Bug: PowerShell module update jobs not validated for failure
description:
## Description

`Update-PowerShellModules` uses background jobs to update modules, but after `Receive-Job` it never checks `$job.State` for failures. A job that errors out is counted as success.

## Impact

**Medium** — Package counts in history/logs may overcount. No functional harm (modules just stay at current version), but misleading reporting.

## Location

`Invoke-BootUpdateCycle.ps1` ~line 629:

```powershell
Receive-Job $job | ForEach-Object { Write-Log $_ }
Remove-Job $job -Force
```

## Suggested Fix

```powershell
$jobOutput = Receive-Job $job -EA SilentlyContinue
if ($jobOutput) { $jobOutput | ForEach-Object { Write-Log $_ } }
if ($job.State -eq 'Failed') {
    Write-Log "Module update job failed" -Level Warn
}
Remove-Job $job -Force
```
close_reason: Closed on GitHub (gh-5) — backfilled into beads

title: Bug: $LASTEXITCODE stale value causes false notification success logs
description:
## Description

The notification code checks `$LASTEXITCODE` after calling `msg.exe`, but `$LASTEXITCODE` may still hold a value from a previous external command. This can produce misleading log entries like "Notification: msg.exe sent" when it actually failed.

## Impact

**Low** — Cosmetic log inaccuracy only. Does not affect update behavior.

## Location

`Invoke-BootUpdateCycle.ps1` ~line 775:

```powershell
if (Test-Path $msgExe) {
    & $msgExe * /TIME:120 "$Title`n`n$Message" 2>$null
    if ($LASTEXITCODE -eq 0) { Write-Log 'Notification: msg.exe sent' }
}
```

## Suggested Fix

Clear before use:

```powershell
$global:LASTEXITCODE = 0
& $msgExe * /TIME:120 "$Title`n`n$Message" 2>$null
if ($LASTEXITCODE -eq 0) { Write-Log 'Notification: msg.exe sent' }
else { Write-Log "msg.exe exited $LASTEXITCODE" -Level Warn }
```
close_reason: Closed on GitHub (gh-4) — backfilled into beads

title: Bug: crash recovery silently ignores unknown phase names
description:
## Description

When recovering from a crash (BSOD, power loss), the crash-recovery logic looks up `$State.LastPhaseStarted` in a `$phaseToFlag` hashtable. If the value is corrupted or unknown, the lookup returns `$null` and the code silently treats the phase as "not done" — no warning is logged.

## Impact

**Medium** — On state corruption, the script silently restarts from an unexpected point. Could lead to phases being re-run or skipped without any log trail explaining why.

## Location

`Invoke-BootUpdateCycle.ps1` ~lines 238-239:

```powershell
$flagName = $phaseToFlag[$State.LastPhaseStarted]
$isDone = if ($flagName -and ($State.PSObject.Properties.Name -contains $flagName)) { [bool]$State.$flagName } else { $false }
```

## Suggested Fix

Log a warning when the phase name is unrecognized:

```powershell
if ($State.LastPhaseStarted -and -not $phaseToFlag.ContainsKey($State.LastPhaseStarted)) {
    Write-Log "WARNING: Unknown phase '$($State.LastPhaseStarted)' in crash recovery — resetting" -Level Warn
    $State.LastPhaseStarted = $null
}
```
close_reason: Closed on GitHub (gh-3) — backfilled into beads

title: Bug: pip JSON parsing breaks on single outdated package
description:
## Description

`Update-PipPackages` pipes `pip list --outdated --format=json` into `ConvertFrom-Json` without wrapping the result in `@()`. When exactly one package is outdated, `ConvertFrom-Json` returns a single PSObject instead of an array.

## Impact

**Medium** — `foreach` still works on a single object in PowerShell, so this doesn't crash. However, if pip returns malformed JSON or an error string, `ConvertFrom-Json -EA SilentlyContinue` returns `$null` and failures are silently swallowed with no log entry.

## Location

`Invoke-BootUpdateCycle.ps1` ~line 584:

```powershell
$outdated = & pip list --outdated --format=json 2>$null | ConvertFrom-Json -EA SilentlyContinue
foreach ($pkg in $outdated) {
    Write-Log "Upgrading: $($pkg.name)"
    & pip install --upgrade $pkg.name 2>&1 | ForEach-Object { Write-Log $_ }
    $count++
}
```

## Suggested Fix

```powershell
$json = & pip list --outdated --format=json 2>$null
if (-not $json) { return @{ Success = $true; Count = 0 } }
$outdated = @($json | ConvertFrom-Json -EA SilentlyContinue)
if (-not $outdated) {
    Write-Log 'pip: failed to parse outdated list' -Level Warn
    return @{ Success = $true; Count = 0 }
}
```
close_reason: Closed on GitHub (gh-2) — backfilled into beads

title: Bug: $PID read-only variable collision crashes all package manager updates
description:
## Description

`Get-ProcessTreeActivity` in `Invoke-BootUpdateCycle.ps1` uses `$pid` as a loop variable (line 387), which collides with PowerShell's read-only automatic variable `$PID` (current process ID). PowerShell variables are case-insensitive.

## Impact

**Critical** — Every package manager phase (winget, choco, etc.) crashes immediately on start with:

```
Cannot overwrite variable PID because it is read-only or constant.
```

The entire update cycle fails at phase 1.

## Root Cause

```powershell
# Line 387 — Get-ProcessTreeActivity BFS loop
$pid = $queue.Dequeue()   # $pid == $PID — read-only
```

## Fix

Rename `$pid` → `$procId` in the BFS loop.

## Status

**Fixed** in v2.0.1 (commit e2c64a8).
close_reason: Closed on GitHub (gh-1) — backfilled into beads
